intro.kicker: Topic 1 of 13 • Chapter: Rotational Motion • Unit: Rotational Motion • Physics
intro.description: A point that moves as though all the mass were concentrated there and all external forces were applied there. This topic covers Definition and Position Vector and Important Points about Centre of Mass. Key principles include: Centre of mass may lie outside the material of the body; In symmetrical bodies with homogenous mass distribution, centre of mass coincides with geometrical centre. In NEET Physics, Centre of Mass is tested directly and as part of multi-concept problems. Mastery requires both conceptual clarity and strong numerical practice.

expectedQuestions.description: Centre of Mass features in NEET Physics as a direct question or embedded in multi-step problems. Detailed study of Rotational Motion is recommended.

timeRequired.description: Theory 10–15 min, plus solving 20 practice problems. Efficient if you know the formula derivation.

difficulty.description: Rotational Motion difficulty level. Mastery requires understanding conditions of validity, dimensional analysis, and numerical practice with NEET-style traps.

nriUsaGap.description: Rotational dynamics depth in Centre of Mass exceeds AP Physics 1 scope. AP Physics C covers it, but most NRI students take AP Physics 1. Full rotational dynamics study is needed for NEET.

weightage.sectionTitle: Centre of Mass — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Centre of Mass appears regularly in NEET Physics; never skip it.
Often combined with adjacent topics in multi-step problems.
Master formula derivation and conceptual application for best results.

strategySteps.title: Concept First
strategySteps.description: Understand the physical meaning behind Centre of Mass. Derive key formulae from first principles so you can reconstruct them under exam pressure.

strategySteps.title: Formula Mapping
strategySteps.description: List all formulae for this topic on a single sheet. Note the conditions under which each applies. NEET frequently tests boundary conditions and exceptions.

strategySteps.title: Practice NEET-style MCQs
strategySteps.description: Solve at least 20 past-paper-style questions on this topic. Note the traps: unit mismatches, sign errors, and direction conventions account for most wrong answers.

subtopics.name: Important Points about Centre of Mass

cards.title: Core Concept: Centre of Mass
cards.coreRevision: Centre of Mass is a key pillar of Rotational Motion in NEET Physics. Understanding its definitions, derivations, and standard results is essential for solving both theory and numerical questions.
points: Centre of mass may lie outside the material of the body
In symmetrical bodies with homogenous mass distribution, centre of mass coincides with geometrical centre
cards.example: Formula: r_cm = (m₁r₁ + m₂r₂ + ... + mₙrₙ)/(m₁ + m₂ + ... + mₙ)

cards.title: NEET Traps to Avoid
cards.coreRevision: NEET setters target conceptual gaps in Centre of Mass. The most common errors involve sign conventions, unit mismatches, and misapplication of conditions under which formulae are valid.
points: Check sign conventions carefully (vector vs scalar quantities)
Verify that all conditions for the formula are satisfied
Dimensional analysis can catch formula errors instantly
cards.example: In NEET, Centre of Mass questions often combine with adjacent chapter concepts. Practice multi-step problems to expose hidden links.

curriculum.hintText: Topics in Centre of Mass covered differently or not at all in AP Physics vs NEET.

gaps.title: Centre of Mass in US vs Indian Curriculum
gaps.description: Rotational dynamics is covered in AP Physics C (Mechanics) but not AP Physics 1. NEET students without calculus background still must master moment of inertia derivations for standard shapes.
bullets: NEET expects derivation-level mastery; AP Physics often treats Centre of Mass as applied only.
NRI students should supplement textbook study with NEET-specific problem sets.
Pay special attention to SI unit standards and sign conventions which differ from US conventions.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes Centre of Mass in the context of NEET Physics?

options.text: Centre of Mass applies only under idealized conditions with no friction or air resistance
Centre of Mass is a fundamental result derivable from first principles and applies within well-defined physical conditions
Centre of Mass has no mathematical formulation and is purely qualitative
Centre of Mass contradicts Newton's laws in non-inertial frames

questions.explanation: Centre of Mass is a rigorously derived physical principle within Rotational Motion. It has a precise mathematical form, applies under specified conditions, and is fully consistent with Newton's laws. NEET frequently tests whether students can distinguish domain of applicability from physical principle validity.

questions.question: In the study of Rotational Motion, which statement about Important Points about Centre of Mass is CORRECT for a NEET Physics student?

options.text: It is only tested as an isolated topic and never combined with energy or momentum problems
The dimensional formula does not follow from SI base units
It is integral to Rotational Motion and appears in direct and multi-concept NEET questions
It is outside the NEET Physics syllabus since 2020

questions.explanation: Important Points about Centre of Mass is a core component of Rotational Motion and regularly appears in NEET. Multi-step problems combining this with energy, momentum, or kinematics are common. Dimensional analysis confirms SI unit consistency. This topic has remained in the NEET syllabus continuously.

questions.question: In a NEET context, when is Centre of Mass most directly applicable?
options: Only in ideal, frictionless, zero-gravity scenarios
Whenever the stated conditions for Centre of Mass are satisfied, including in standard NEET problems
questions.answer: Whenever the stated conditions for Centre of Mass are satisfied, including in standard NEET problems

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the dimensional formula of the primary physical quantity in Centre of Mass?
options: It follows from SI base units with standard MLT notation
It requires non-SI units for proper expression
Dimensional analysis is inapplicable to this quantity
questions.answer: It follows from SI base units with standard MLT notation

questions.question: The most common NEET trap in Centre of Mass problems is:
options: Applying the formula outside its domain of validity or with incorrect sign conventions
questions.answer: Applying the formula outside its domain of validity or with incorrect sign conventions

items.title: Define Centre of Mass precisely
items.description: Write out the complete definition of Centre of Mass including all qualifying conditions and the physical quantities involved.

items.title: Derive the key formula from first principles
items.description: Reproduce the main derivation without notes. Understanding each step ensures you can identify which formula applies under non-standard NEET conditions.

items.title: State conditions of validity
items.description: List all assumptions and conditions under which Centre of Mass results hold. NEET setters test these boundaries.

items.title: Master Definition and Position Vector
items.description: Solve at least 5 NEET-style problems specifically on Definition and Position Vector to ensure pattern recognition and speed.

items.title: Master Important Points about Centre of Mass
items.description: Solve at least 5 NEET-style problems specifically on Important Points about Centre of Mass to ensure pattern recognition and speed.

items.question: How many questions from Centre of Mass appear in NEET each year?
items.answer: Centre of Mass typically contributes 0–2 questions per NEET paper depending on the year. While it may not appear every year, it often features in multi-concept problems linked to adjacent topics in Rotational Motion. Skipping it is risky.

items.question: Is Centre of Mass in the NEET 2025 syllabus?
items.answer: Yes, Centre of Mass remains part of the NEET Physics syllabus under Rotational Motion. The National Medical Commission periodically updates the syllabus, so always verify with the latest NMC/NTA official syllabus notification before your exam.

items.question: What is the best way to revise Centre of Mass before NEET?
items.answer: Use a three-pass strategy: (1) Read the derivation and concept; (2) Solve 10 NEET-style MCQs; (3) On the day before exam, review only the formulae sheet and your flagged error patterns from practice. Focus on conditions of validity and sign conventions.

items.question: What is the most common mistake in Centre of Mass problems?
items.answer: The most frequent errors in Centre of Mass are: applying the formula outside its domain (e.g., using ideal-gas results for real gases), sign errors in vector quantities, and ignoring units in multi-step calculations. Practice specifically designed trap questions to inoculate against these errors.

grounding_summary: Centre of Mass is covered in the assigned page range of pages/physics-1/ as part of Rotational Motion.
The local OCR pages establish definitions, key concepts, and formulae for Centre of Mass in the NEET Physics context.
Chapter research from Exa confirms current NEET exam patterns and authoritative sources for Centre of Mass.
grounding_excerpt: Up to now we have studied translatory motion of a point mass. In this chapter we will study the rotatory motion of rigid body about a fixed axis.
1. Rigid body: A rigid body is a body that can rotate with all the parts locked together and without any change in its shape.
2. System: A collection of any number of particles interacting with one another and are under consideration during analysis of a situation are said to form a system.
4. External forces: To move or stop an object of finite size, we have to apply a force on the object from outside. This exerted force on a given system is called an external force.
